titleStmt.title: From one garden to the other (in 24 hours)

body.head: From one garden to the other (in 24 hours)
body.p: 10 May and it’s time to leave 111 Victoria Road for my other garden, not tended since March, and therefore in dire need of attention. So I lock up the house carefully and set off on the dot of 1430. I have my minimalist backpack, packed with all that is needful in the way of tickets and passport and such like; I am wearing a nice khaki jacket which has languished too long in the cupboard and some new brown shoes from Gueret, in which I am less confident, but they deserve a chance… The sun shines benevolently on Victoria Road as I saunter to the bus stop, and within five minutes up rolls that comparative rarity, a 700 bus.

I don’t appreciate the journey as I am busy trying to book a bus for the final stage of this journey and the Service Mobilité du Grand Gueret (aka le TAD) for some reason is not answering its phone. And goes on not answering when I get to Oxford Parkway 10 minutes later, when I board the 1502 departure to Oxford, and indeed at each subsequent stop for the rest of the day, as I keep hopefully trying at every opportunity.

figure.head: Today I am getting on where I usually get off

body.p: Mysterious, and not a little frustrating, since my planned itinerary has me arriving at Gueret station at 1735 tomorrow, which is just right for the last TAD of the day, but completely useless in every other respect. Have I got the wrong phone number? Are they on strike? This is the sort of thing that spoils an otherwise perfect journey, sidling around North Oxford by train for a change instead of lurching down the Banbury Road by bus. And here is my 1530 cross country train to Southampton Airport Parkway, on time and not too crowded, and I sit on it for an hour getting more and more annoyed by the absence of response from Gueret, instead of enjoying the cross country experience.

At the airport, which is only 99 steps from the train station as a notice helpfully reminds me, I saunter through security, with plenty of time to go and get a coffee upstairs, before buying a vegan sandwich and a bilious green drink to eat on the plane. The man in the shop points out that I could get this “meal deal” for 2 pounds less if I add a packet of crisps, so I ask him to get me a packet of crisps, and then give it to him since I hate crisps. I ponder this retail marketing madness and then Go To My Gate (i.e. cross the floor to a different chair). There are only a dozen or so people waiting for this flight, but we still have to be processed through the door onto a bus. Which cannot move until all the people walking in front of it from some other flight have safely crossed. And so into the little sardine tin in the sky, with all the other sardines. Half the seats on the plane are empty, so I move to one of the more remote ones as soon as the door is shut and do some not too stressful markup of a Swiss French 19th century novel to pass the time. Bonjour Limoges Bellegard! All is much as it was the last time I saw it but this time I am going to explore its hinterland. For Mr Google assures me that across the car park, there is a bijou hotel, the Citotel Alizes, a one storey building of modest dimensions, where one can get a bed for the night though not much else.

figure.head: Citotel Alizes in the cold hard light of morning

body.p: I think it is owned by the same company as the airport’s posh restaurant. No matter: the nice lady at reception provides me with all I require for the moment, namely viz a bottle of beer, and the loan of a phone charger (what? Did I really leave my phone charger behind in Oxford? Yes, dear reader, I did. Fortunately my destination is replete with such things). Having given up on the TAD for tomorrow, plan B is to hunt down an outrageously expensive taxi, rather than exploit Lisa’s motor, since she isn’t available anyway. And so to bed.

Next morning, the TAD answers its phone and gives me the simple and obvious explanation for yesterday’s debacle: it was a jour férié, a public holiday, as any (French) fool would know, so naturally there was no one there. And no, out of the question of course to arrange a tad for today today; it has to be for tomorrow. So I arrange one for tomorrow, thinking at least I will get some tomato plants in Gueret market. And checking today’s trains, I discover that the one I was planning to take in order to connect with the TAD has been cancelled anyway. A reason to be cheerful, and even more so to accept Lisa’s kind offer of a lift with some unknown friend of hers, assuming I can get to Gueret a bit earlier. Which I certainly can, for I have a cunning plan. All this before a rather dull French breakfast, after which I pay the bill, and set off for the first part of the cunning plan. Mr Google says it can be done, and the Limoge bus company agrees. There is a bus stop just outside the hotel, but buses stop there only 3 times a day, all inconvenient. Much more interestingly, there is another bus stop down the road some 800 metres away, where buses also stop 4 or 5 times a day, but at more useful times.

figure.head: Here’s where Ave de l’Aeroport gets sneaky

body.p: So off I plod, down Avenue de l’Aeroport, which is surprised to see a pedestrian but makes room for me all the same, even providing a helpful crossing point when the road hits one of the many roundabouts which France decrees must accompany every major intersection. This all goes remarkably smoothly: also it’s still a lovely sunny day, which is good, as I arrive at the bus stop in question a good 20 minutes too early. And lo, exactly on time at 1046, the promised EX1 bus hoves into view and picks me up.

It is a luxurious coach with one other passenger on it, and it whisks me away to the centre of Limoges for a mere 1 euro 50 (i.e. approx 24 euros less than a taxi). I stroll through Limoges feeling very smug, and with time in hand to do my chores. These include buying some toothpaste, getting in food for the next few days, but firstly sorting out my Carte Senior at the magnificent Gare de Limoges Benedictines.

figure.head: What is it about this photo which got so many likes on Instagram?

body.p: Inside, I have to queue up for what feels like forever, but is only about 10 minutes before I can get someone to help resolve the “coquille” somewhere in my dossier on the SNCF computer. The unflappable cheminot regulates the affair sans problème and sells me a ticket to Gueret as well. And then I walk round the corner to the Grand Frais supermarket to buy some provisions: sadly they have run out of milk, and don’t sell bread at all, which I should have remembered. But they do sell nice fresh vegetables. And so back to the architectural joys of Limoges station in good time for my train, and thinking about lunch. But what fresh torture is this?

figure.head: The post modern station buffet is no more

body.p: Alas, the station buffet has closed down seemingly forever; somewhat chastened I have no choice but to buy a horrible industrial sandwich from the station mewsagent and eat it sitting outside in the sunshine. The 1311 TER to Gueret is at the platform already, and ready to trundle along via Bonnat, Saint Sulpice Le Laurier (where it changes direction), Vielleville, Montaigut etc as usual, though this time with a minor excitement en route. They are renewing the caténaire, I learned from the guard, and people were still on the line, so we had to stop. Being at the front I witnessed the driver descending from the train to shout at a trackside phone, then returning to get an instruction manual or page of secret codes from his bag in the cab, then going back to shout at it some more before proceeding gingerly through a red light.

Anyway, we arrived in Gueret on time, and here is Lisa and her new friend Debbie, and Debbie’s rather splendid red Rover to greet me and drive me home in style, in return for a cup of tea and some petrol money. Debbie comes from the West Country, as is evident in her speech, and has just bought a house down the road in Monneger, where she spent the winter. I also learn that she is or was a professional musician, (double bass, but none of that jazz nonsense; we are talking proper orchestra here) and find myself feeling unusually well disposed to her amiable doggy.

figure.head: Plenty of work to do in this garden

body.p: And after they leave I determine that there is a clear need for urgent attention to the jungle that the garden has become, but put it off till tomorrow. It’s been a long journey : 24 hours exactly, bearing in mind le décalage horaire.
